segments.text: Well, welcome to the Environment Select Committee this

Conservation Authority. Uh, we welcome you here.

As you're aware, EECA is the government's authority on energy
efficiency and its delivery arm on the related
programs. It's been globally proven that energy
efficiency is instrumental in achieving more with less
(1) and system, and provides better
outcomes. I'm pleased to share with you today
EECA's achievements for '24/'25, a year in which
EECA sharpened its focus to align with the government's priorities
of strengthening energy security and improving energy
affordability. The change in government's direction
last year set a very clear expectation for EECA to
concentrate on practical, market-based measures that
reduce pressure on the energy system and to help keep costs
down. In response, EECA streamlined its
programs and prioritized the highest value interventions
and accelerated work that directly supports greater security of
supply and lower energy bills for New
Zealanders. EECA's key role is on the demand
side of the energy system. Reducing unnecessary
energy use is one of the most cost-effective ways to improve
reliability and affordability.
Every unit of energy
eases pressure on the system and helps avoid higher costs to
consumers. EECA's regulatory role is central to
this. In '24/'25, EECA's energy
efficiency standards and regulations delivered more than 1.5
petajoules of savings, which is equivalent to the annual use of
power in over 36,000 homes, and it's
around $37 million in national benefits.
The recently approved modernization of EECA's legislative framework will
enable these two tools to expand even further to support
system resilience and consumer
affordability. Alongside the
regulations, EECA continues to provide practical advice to
inform households and businesses on how to reduce their energy
costs and manage demand more effectively, particularly
during peak periods when the network is under the greatest
strength. These well-targeted information
programs have actually demonstrated impact.
For instance, the Switch On Efficiency campaign
delivered in the order of $2.50
return for every dollar invested, and the more recent
Winter Energy Campaign actually had a bit higher return
than that.
EECA continues to work with industry to help bring forward new
technologies, demand flexibility solutions, and new, new
fuels into, such as woody biomass and biogas,
because they offer real opportunities to reduce operating cost,
defer supply-side investment, and strengthen system
resilience. Looking ahead, EECA has
realigned its '25/'26 program to focus squarely
on where it can deliver the greatest impact, improving energy
affordability, and supporting a reliable and resis-resilient
energy system, and contribute to New Zealand's economic
wellbeing. We welcome any questions you may
have of us.
